speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] And I was there maybe a few weeks, then they let us go. [INAUDIBLE] soldiers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go any further, when we  have the thing on tape, that's when you tell me the stories and [INAUDIBLE] For what I'm doing right now, uh-huh. All I need is a shopping list from you, bum, bum, bum, bum. [INTERPOSING VOICES] So here's what I need to know, OK, from Stalag V-A you went to Lublin.

words.text: you tell me the stories and

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there how long? Three weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Lublin is in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland. And it was like a holding camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after being released from POW.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. Now, from three weeks after here, where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 9, 1996. The survivor being interviewed is Rosalie Steinweis. The interviewer is Steve Greenberg. We're in North Miami Beach, Florida, USA. And this interview will be conducted in English. And this is the first tape. Today is January 9, 1996. I'm Steve Greenberg. I'll be interviewing Rosalie Steinweis. We're in North Miami Beach, Florida, USA. And we'll be conducting this interview in English. Don't start yet. Can you say and spell your name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-S-A-L-I-E, Rosalie, Steinweis, S-T-E-I-N-W-E-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What's your earliest memory before the war? What can you remember about life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was more or less family life. I'm one of five children. There was grandparents, aunts and uncles, cousins, a typical Jewish life, you know, close family relationships. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But financially, things were up and down. I would say normal, like everywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What'd you like to do as a young girl growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to school. I went through seven grades of school. And higher education in Poland was not accessible to everybody, not easily accessible to everybody. So most children will learn a trade. And I was taught sewing, tailoring. And that was until 1939, when-- when things started changing. Actually, things started changing, you know, like you would say the wind of war, when we heard what was going on in Germany. And we saw refugees coming after the Kristallnacht 1938. And being that we lived close to the German border, we knew that sooner or later, things are going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism growing up? Did you see it? Did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Poland, it was a daily occurrence. It was a way of life. We knew that we were hated. We were considered, even in-- in our own mind, we knew that we are-- I wouldn't say that we were. But we always thought of ourselves as being second-class citizens. Because we grew up with hatred. Even children were being beaten up. And the word-- the word Jew did not mean just being Jewish. It meant insult. It was always referred to us as-- as, you know, a derogatory way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind-- you said it was a daily occurrence. What kind of things happened that were daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- we-- we encountered anti-Semitism in every step of life, even at school. Even at school, yes? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] children were being thrown rocks at. Jewish businesses were being boycotted. They were-- they were organized in certain places where they were marching in front of Jewish stores with posters. And the main slogan was-- may I use it in Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Means, don't buy in Jewish stores. And windows were being broken. It was-- that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- as a kid growing up, what did you find? Did the other kids treat you differently? Did teachers treat you differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, more or less, we stuck to Jewish children. In fact, the public school that I went to was a Jewish class. There was several classes of the same grade, but the Jewish children were in a separate group, a separate class of Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No Jewish teacher. Even the teachers expressed anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? They used to-- like, for instance, our names were not-- in America, Jewish names are more or less Americanized. In Poland, you could not-- my name, my Jewish name is Rosa Leah. I-- eventually later, on I combined it into one name, Rosalie. But at that time, I couldn't do that. I had to go under my Yiddish name, Rosa Leah. And all these little things added up to-- in fact, maybe we didn't even feel that we are being discriminated in that particular way. But that's just the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in your home, your house? What was your mother like, your father like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were hardworking parents, of course, raising five children. My father was a tailor by profession. But eventually, he was able to bring himself up to a point when he was able to have a business where he had people employed. As-- he used to prepare the work for people to do, and to sew. And eventually, he was able to sell it from his own establishment. And things were well for-- for a long time. And being the oldest child, I kind-- kind of always felt a responsibility and always had a lot of interests and things going on. And I was always aware of-- of changes in-- in-- in-- in everything. In like 1929, when the depression came, I was still a little girl. But I knew that financial situation has changed and things became more difficult. And there was a time when my father lost a business. There was a lot of unpaid bills. And for a certain length of time, he moved to a different part of the country where he was able to sell with some of the merchandise. And all our-- all us children remained in Kielce, where we were born, with our mother. And sooner or later, things didn't turn out very well. Because my father had to come back. He wasn't able to make a success in another town. And then we all moved to a town called Myslowice, which is Upper Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been-- I would not remember whether I was 8 or 9 at the time. That could have been maybe 1931 or '32, something like that. And there my father worked in his own establishment as a tailor, as a custom tailor. And we children went to school. And that was going on more or less normal until 1939 on September 1st when war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were having discussions at the dinner table and whatnot, was their talk of-- you know, did your-- were your parents preparing you? Was there any kind of talk? Or, was it ignored? What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not ignored, because we were very much aware of the goings-on in Germany at that time. You know, the newspapers were full of it. And the German Jews who were deported after the Kristallnacht, they had-- they didn't have far to go. Because we-- I don't remember exactly how close we were to the German border, but it was not too far away. And this is the first place that these people came to. And as children, we didn't have to be told anything. We could just see it from observation. And we knew that these people were taken out in the middle of the night. And they were herded into trains and brought to the Polish border. Actually, most of these people were of Polish origin, and they lived in Germany without being German citizens. And they-- they called them the [NON-ENGLISH], meaning Jews of Eastern origin. And later on, they were able to-- to claim their belongings, but they weren't able to go back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were being dumped from Germany into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would see them and talk to them. And they would say--

words.text: see them and talk to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.  We did. We made friends with them. They-- they came to school with us. And later on-- that was 1938, so like close to the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- was the shul-- before the war, what was religious life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious life was-- of course, people were religious, very religious and less religious. Somehow if you would want to compare it to religiousness in America, we would say that even the people who were less religious in Poland would be considered religious here. Because everybody observed kashrut, most everybody anyway. We attended synagogue. And I wouldn't even say that there was a reformed Judaism in Poland. Because if there was, we didn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the synagogue very aware before the war of what was going on? Did it make-- did the rabbi make sermons about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you remember hearing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard that we were in danger. That's what we heard. Because Hitler didn't make any secret of his intentions. And he always wanted to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] he always wanted to spread his country. They-- they were talking about [NON-ENGLISH], meaning that they needed more space in which to live. And they always had a claim on certain part of-- of-- on the western part of Poland. And so we always knew. We somehow knew that someday something bad is going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- what was the first inkling that the war was coming towards you? What was your first memory of things changing in town? What did you see happening around you, when you said, uh-oh, it's about to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, this part of Poland that we lived was once-- if you know Polish history more or less, Poland was being torn apart for many-- for three times, actually. And certain parts of Poland were incorporated into Germany, Russia, and Austria. This particular part was sometimes Germany, sometimes Poland. After the armistice in 1918, this part was given to Germany. There was a lot of German people there. But the Poles made several uprisings. And in the end, it became Poland. But there were still many German people living there. And so, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, we-- we-- I really lost my train of thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. Where did you see your-- what did you see change in the town just before the war came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you notice that told you, uh-oh, we're about to go into something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we could notice that the German-- that the real German people who-- who remained there, that these people were kind of acting up like they knew something. And then we also noticed that some young people who came from German parents disappeared. They ran into Germany to join up with Hitler. In fact, later on when they did come back, they came back wearing Nazi uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what was happening just as the war was about to start for you personally? What was going on in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really wouldn't [INAUDIBLE]. I was a typical teenage girl. And somehow, teenagers don't react to-- to all these political changes. So when it came, we just accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing that happened to your family, your home, as the war started up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually the war started on a Friday, September 1. And we saw-- actually, it wasn't me. My mother and my younger sister went someplace. And they saw a German airplane approaching the border, crossing actually, crossing the border, and-- and just continuing. Poland was not very well prepared to fight war against Germany. And on the third day, which was Sunday, we all escaped. Because we were afraid of what they'll do to us as Jews once they catch us. In fact, the Poles were more or less a little bit cocky. They-- they were confident that they'll be able to defy the German army. But as we all saw, they weren't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where'd you go? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to a town called Sosnowiec, which was about 6 kilometers away. We walked there. We carried whatever we could. And there, we-- we went on trains, not real passenger trains. We went on these open--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cargo?

speaker: interviewee
text: --cargo trains, right. And-- and we went wherever we went, deeper into Poland, going east. We were planning to join the rest of-- of my father's relatives who lived in Kielce, where I was born. But we weren't able to get there, because we were being shot at from German planes. They did not throw bombs. But they were flying low and shooting from the planes. And that was-- we left on Sunday. And then we came to a little town called [INAUDIBLE], which was like between Myslowice and Kielce. And we-- we couldn't go any further. So we left the train, and we went into town. We didn't have-- we didn't know anybody to go to, so we slept like in the open fields. And then the next morning, we were going to continue walking to Kielce. But all of a sudden, from every corner, wherever you looked there were German soldiers approaching. They were there before us. And, of course, we weren't able to continue. And we went back. We were able to return to Myslowice in three days, all of us except our father, because as we came closer, we met people who told us that all Jewish men were put into prison. They didn't bother the women and the children, only the men. So my father stayed away. And we came back home. We were not welcomed very badly, but they had to accept us. They let us into our own home. By the time we got home, we discovered that we had been robbed of certain items. But there was nobody to complain to, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in our home. And then my father, we were able to meet him at a certain bridge, not to talk to him, but just to see him. Because they posted a guard on a certain bridge that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] above a river, which was a border once before between the German part where we lived in Myslowice and the Russian part, which was Russian then, but Poland later. And so he would come close to that river. And we would see each other from across the bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow in November, we were able to acquire for him proof of residency. Because we all had-- we all had-- we were registered in city hall. Every resident had to be registered in city hall. And so we were able to acquire a proof of identity. And he was able to cross the bridge and come home. And we lived more or less normally for a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- we were receiving rations. But then everybody was, Polacks too-- Poles, I beg your pardon. And-- in Polish, you say Polack. So if it sounds derogatory, I don't mean it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody received rations for food. But the Jews received smaller rations. Some of the cards were cut down to smaller rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made the best that we could. My father was able to work at home. And we were able to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue going to school during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Everything that you-- we weren't able to go to the movies. It was-- there was a sign saying [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, Jews not welcome. The same thing was being put out in front of the sports arenas. You couldn't go ice skating or anything. We just had to stay put. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during that whole time then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing. We lived. We existed. We-- we couldn't-- there was a curfew. We couldn't be out after 10:00. And some people were civil to us, because we lived with them so many years. And, of course, there was still Poles in the town. They were not deported. They were allowed to live. But we didn't know that they were having plans for us, to evacuate us. In 1940, for April 20th, which was Hitler's birthday, they promised to make Upper Silesia, which was called Oberschlesien in German. They were going-- they were-- they were going to make it [NON-ENGLISH], which means, free of Jews and to give Hitler an Upper Silesia free of Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all had to leave. The majority of the people went to a town called [INAUDIBLE]. Us in particular, with another family, whose-- our parents and this-- these parents were friends. And we were friends with the teenage children.

speaker: interviewee
text: These people originally came from Oswiecim, which later became Auschwitz. And we received a permit to go there with them. And we-- we were able to find an apartment in a house of a Pole. And we lived there. And my father was able to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family, this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like there? Tell me a little bit about life there for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was more or less-- more or less normal for a while. Of course, the food was rationed, like before. And it was more or less peaceful. But what we saw going out into the streets-- by the way, I forgot to mention that we were required to wear armbands. At that time, it was a blue-- a white armband with a blue Star of David. It was about three inches wide. And we were required to wear it I think on my left arm if I'm not mistaken. And going out into the street, we had more or less enough freedom during the day. And we-- going out into the street, we could see SS people, I would call it, escorting at prisoners who were wearing these pajama-type uniforms. And they would take them out to clean the streets, sweep the streets, march them to do menial labor, from time to time. We didn't see them all the time. But later, little by little, we saw more, more and more of those. In fact, comes into my memory a certain SS man who distinguished himself by being very tall. He was so tall that-- unusually tall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was very-- how shall I say? He used to yell at the prisoners. And he would poke them with the butt of the rifle for nothing at all. And somehow, still being young, we didn't-- we didn't know what time will bring. And then 1941-- Well, [INAUDIBLE] during that time, you were still with your family?

words.text: you were still with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were able to get food once we had money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd go out and buy bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would go out and buy, of course, with ration cards, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of foods were you eating? What were you doing all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the food was-- of course, we didn't have-- there wasn't everything. But there were vegetables. There were vegetables. There were bread. There was some meat. In fact, my father, being that he worked at his tailoring profession, he-- we-- we had some customers who were butchers. And we were able to get meat beside the rations. You know, you have some connections, you are able to manage. And somehow we-- we did pretty good. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you thinking this is as bad as it was going to get. Or, did you feel it was going to get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not-- we didn't-- as young people, you don't anticipate terrible-- a terrible future. You just take it day by day. And we just-- we just waited to see what's going to happen. But then in their winter of 1941, they deported the Poles first. All the Poles were deported. They just were notified one day that they have to leave the next day. And they just had to leave. In fact, the town where we lived in then, Oswiecim, that part of the town was before the river Sola, which later on became Birkenau, which in Polish was called Brzezinka That's where the railroad station was, where they later brought in all these people. Anyway, a few weeks-- didn't know what to think about it, since they deported Poles and they left Jews. Somehow it was like curious. And then in March, they notified the Jews that they have to move out of this part of town and go across the river into the main town of Oswiecim, which we had to do. We had no way to move. We carried pieces of furniture. Everybody carried a different thing. I remember I carried a mirror across the river. We took apart the beds, and every child took a different piece of furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back and forth, or just went once?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just-- well, took it-- we went on foot. We-- we-- no, we took it into town. And the Jewish committee, which was called [NON-ENGLISH], arranged for accommodations, for living accommodations. And we were given a place to live with a family of three who had-- they-- they had to do it. They had to give us place to live. They gave us a room. And we stayed there, oh, I would say maybe a month. And then came the big shock. Then we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I-- I-- this is a good place probably to stop before we go forward, so let's stop right there.

words.text: stop before we go forward, so let's

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll pick--
